pattern: A fixed-size sliding window over the last m values, partitioned into smallest-k, middle, and largest-k buckets with a running middle sum, lets the trimmed mean be read off in logarithmic time per update.
hints: Only the most recent m elements ever matter, so maintain them as a sliding window and drop the oldest each time the window overflows.
The answer ignores the k smallest and k largest of those m values; if you keep a running sum of just the middle band, the average is that sum divided by m minus two k.

Use an ordered/balanced structure (or three buckets kept sorted) so inserting and removing a value, and rebalancing across the smallest-k / middle / largest-k boundaries, is logarithmic; before m elements exist return minus one.

edge_inputs.description: statement example: returns -1 before m elements, then the trimmed average
smallest legal m with k=1 so exactly one middle element remains
all equal values: trimmed average equals that value
window slides past older extremes; result must reflect only the last m
result rounds down (floor) when the trimmed sum is not divisible
k larger than 1 trims a wider band on both ends
duplicate values straddling the trim boundary must be counted correctly

stress.description: long stream of additions interleaved with average queries over a moderate window
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = rng.randint(3, 40)
    kmax = (m - 1) // 2
    k = rng.randint(1, max(1, kmax))
    ops = ['MKAverage']
    args = [[m, k]]
    for _ in range(size):
        if rng.random() < 0.7:
            ops.append('addElement')
            args.append([rng.randint(1, 1000)])
        else:
            ops.append('calculateMKAverage')
            args.append([])
    return [ops, args]
stress.note: k chosen so 1 < 2k < m always holds; queries before m elements exercise the -1 branch
